effectiveDateNote: Act A1727 has THREE commencement phases, not a single effective date. Phase 1 (1 Jan 2025): administrative/structural amendments. Phase 2 (1 Apr 2025): Commissioner powers, enforcement, penalties. Phase 3 (1 Jun 2025): data portability, right to erasure, data breach notification.

commencementPhases.sections: Sections related to Commissioner appointment, administrative matters
Sections related to enforcement notices, penalties, compounding

commencementPhases.description: Data portability, right to erasure, data breach notification
commencementPhases.sections: New Parts IIIA (data portability), IVA (erasure), VIA (breach notification)

summary: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1727) is the first significant amendment to the PDPA since its original commencement in 2013. Key changes include mandatory data breach notification, data protection officer requirements, expanded cross-border transfer mechanisms, enhanced data subject rights (portability, erasure, restriction, withdrawal), data protection impact assessments, records of processing, enhanced penalties, and expanded Commissioner powers.

changes.description: Expanded definitions to include: 'biometric data', 'data breach', 'data protection officer', 'health data'. Updated definition of 'consent' to require it to be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
General Principle updated to clarify lawful bases for processing, particularly the legitimate interests basis, aligning more closely with modern data protection standards.
Notice and Choice Principle enhanced to require more detailed information notices, including information about data subject rights, data portability, and automated decision-making.

Security Principle updated to explicitly require data users to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, and to have procedures for detecting, reporting, and investigating data breaches.
Enhanced penalties for processing without registration increased to RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.
Direct marketing provisions strengthened: data users must now provide clear opt-out mechanisms in all marketing communications. Opt-out must be as easy as opting in. Enhanced penalties apply.

Cross-border transfer provisions updated to reference new section 129A which provides expanded transfer mechanisms including standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules, and certification schemes.
Enforcement notice provisions updated to include power to order data breach remediation and to require notification to data subjects.
Right of access enhanced to include information about automated decision-making, profiling, and the existence of data portability rights.
Compensation provisions updated to include class action mechanism allowing multiple data subjects to bring a joint claim. Non-material damages (including distress) explicitly included.
Rectification provisions updated to reduce response time requirement from 21 days to 14 days and to require proactive notification to third parties who have received the corrected data.
Fee provisions for access requests updated. Commissioner given power to prescribe zero fee for certain categories of requests, particularly initial requests.

Children's data provisions significantly enhanced. Age of child defined at 18 years. Enhanced penalties (RM500,000 and/or 3 years). Requirement for data users to implement age verification measures where they know or ought to know services are likely to be used by children.

New right to data portability introduced. Data subjects may request personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format. Where technically feasible, data may be transmitted directly to another data user. Applies to data processed by automated means on basis of consent or contract. 21-day compliance deadline.

New express right to withdraw consent introduced. Withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent. Data user must cease processing on receipt of withdrawal notice unless compelling legitimate grounds exist. Withdrawal does not affect prior lawful processing.

New right to erasure (right to be forgotten) introduced. Grounds: data no longer necessary; consent withdrawn; unlawful processing; objection upheld; legal compliance. Where data made public, data user must notify other processors of erasure request. Exceptions for freedom of expression, legal compliance, public interest.

New right to restrict processing introduced. Data subjects may require processing to be limited to storage only while accuracy is disputed, legitimacy assessed, legal claims pending, or objection considered. Data user must notify before lifting restriction.
Commissioner's functions expanded to include overseeing data breach notifications, monitoring DPO compliance, maintaining guidance on DPIAs, and international cooperation on data protection.

Commissioner's powers expanded to include power to conduct unannounced inspections in cases of suspected serious breach, issue binding guidance, and impose financial penalties directly (in addition to referring for prosecution).
Annual report requirements expanded to include statistics on data breach notifications received, DPO registration compliance, DPIA assessments reviewed, and cross-border transfer approvals.
Financial penalty notice provisions significantly enhanced. Maximum penalty that may be imposed via notice increased to reflect the enhanced maximum fines for offences.

Order for financial penalty enhanced. Commissioner may now impose financial penalties of up to RM1 million for serious contraventions. Additional factors (cooperation, financial benefit from breach) added to penalty assessment criteria.

General offences enhanced penalties: maximum fine increased to RM500,000 (from RM300,000) and maximum imprisonment increased to 3 years (from 2 years). Continuing offences attract RM1,000 per day additional fine.
Offences relating to personal data: maximum penalties enhanced to RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment. New offence of obtaining personal data by deception added.

New comprehensive cross-border transfer provision. Expands permitted transfer mechanisms beyond adequacy decisions to include: Commissioner-approved standard contractual clauses (SCCs), binding corporate rules (BCRs), certification schemes, and international arrangements. Commissioner to publish and maintain list of approved mechanisms. Contravention: RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

New mandatory Data Protection Officer (DPO) provision. Prescribed class data users must appoint a DPO. DPO functions: advise on obligations, monitor compliance, cooperate with Commissioner, act as data subject contact point. DPO must have adequate resources, data access, and independence. Failure to appoint: RM250,000 fine and/or 2 years imprisonment.

Corporate liability provisions enhanced: courts may impose the greater of RM3 million or 10% of annual Malaysian turnover in addition to or instead of standard fines, targeting large organisations proportionately.

New provision on liability of officers of body corporate. Directors, CEOs, managers, and similar officers are personally liable for PDPA offences committed by the body corporate with their consent, connivance, or due to their neglect. Officers may be prosecuted independently of the body corporate.

Power to make regulations expanded to cover: data breach notification procedures and forms; DPO qualifications and requirements; DPIA requirements; records of processing; data portability format standards; age verification requirements for children's data.

New offence of unauthorized sale of personal data. Selling, offering to sell, or procuring the sale of unlawfully obtained personal data is an offence. Knowingly purchasing such data is also an offence. Penalties up to RM1,000,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

New mandatory Data Protection Officer (DPO) provision. Prescribed class data users must appoint a DPO. DPO functions: advise on obligations, monitor compliance, cooperate with Commissioner, act as data subject contact point. DPO must have adequate resources, data access, and independence. Details must be registered with Commissioner and published. Failure to appoint: RM500,000 fine. DPO may be internal or external.

New mandatory Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) provision. Required for high-risk processing activities. High-risk indicators: new technologies, large-scale processing, sensitive data, systematic monitoring. Prior consultation with Commissioner required where residual high risk cannot be mitigated. DPIA must include: processing description, necessity assessment, risk assessment, mitigation measures. Failure to conduct required DPIA: RM500,000 fine. Transitional period: 18 months for existing processing activities.

New mandatory Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) provision. Prescribed class data users must maintain comprehensive records including: data user and DPO details, processing purposes, data categories, recipients, cross-border transfers and safeguards, retention periods, security measures description. Must be made available to Commissioner on request. Failure to maintain: RM300,000 fine. Transitional period: 12 months.

New mandatory data breach notification provision — the single most operationally significant change in the 2024 Amendment. Requirements: (1) notify Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach likely to cause significant harm; (2) notify affected data subjects as soon as practicable. Commissioner notification must include: breach description, categories and number of data subjects and records affected, DPO contact details, likely consequences, remedial measures. Phased notifications permitted. Data subject notification must be in plain language and include self-protection recommendations. Delay permitted if notification would impede criminal investigation (law enforcement request required). All breaches must be recorded, including those not requiring notification. Failure to comply: RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

Transitional provisions updated to accommodate Amendment Act requirements: 12-month transition for DPO appointments; 12-month transition for records of processing; 18-month transition for DPIAs on existing processing; deemed compliance period for existing registrations; 6-month deadline for Commissioner to publish Amendment Act compliance guidance.

keyThemes.description: The 2024 Amendment significantly expanded data subject rights, adding rights to data portability (s41), withdrawal of consent (s42), erasure (s43), and restriction of processing (s44), bringing Malaysian data protection law closer to international standards.
New section 143A introduces a 72-hour mandatory data breach notification regime — the first of its kind in Malaysian law. This represents the most operationally significant change for data users.
New obligations around DPIAs (s137) and records of processing (s138) introduce accountability and privacy-by-design principles, requiring proactive rather than reactive compliance.

Maximum fines increased from RM300,000 to RM500,000 (and up to RM1 million for serious cases under s121). Imprisonment increased to 3 years. Corporate penalties linked to turnover (RM3 million or 10% of turnover). These changes are intended to ensure penalties are proportionate and dissuasive for large organisations.

New section 129A expands permitted cross-border transfer mechanisms beyond the original adequacy list to include SCCs, BCRs, certification schemes, and international arrangements, providing more practical options for international business.

Mandatory DPO appointments (s136), DPIA requirements (s137), and records of processing obligations (s138) establish an accountability framework requiring data users to demonstrate compliance proactively.
